message.content:
List all files in these two directories and show me their contents:
1. C:\Users\mbrow\.claude\skills\
2. C:\Users\mbrow\git\gpsl-cp-system\.claude\skills\

For each skill file, show the full file path and contents. I need to understand what skills exist in both places so I can plan a merge.

content.text: I'll explore both directories to list all files and show their contents.

input.command: find "C:\Users\mbrow\.claude\skills" -type f 2>/dev/null || echo "Directory not found or empty"
input.description: List all files in the first skills directory

input.command: find "C:\Users\mbrow\git\gpsl-cp-system\.claude\skills" -type f 2>/dev/null || echo "Directory not found or empty"
input.description: List all files in the second skills directory

content.text: Now let me read the contents of these three skill files:

content.content:
1→---
     2→name: nano-banana
     3→description: REQUIRED for all image generation requests. Generate and edit images using Nano Banana (Gemini CLI). Handles blog featured images, YouTube thumbnails, icons, diagrams, patterns, illustrations, photos, visual assets, graphics, artwork, pictures. Use this skill whenever the user asks to create, generate, make, draw, design, or edit any image or visual content.
     4→allowed-tools: Bash(gemini:*)
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Nano Banana Image Generation
     8→
     9→Generate professional images via the Gemini CLI's nanobanana extension.
    10→
    11→## When to Use This Skill
    12→
    13→ALWAYS use this skill when the user:
    14→- Asks for any image, graphic, illustration, or visual
    15→- Wants a thumbnail, featured image, or banner
    16→- Requests icons, diagrams, or patterns
    17→- Asks to edit, modify, or restore a photo
    18→- Uses words like: generate, create, make, draw, design, visualize
    19→
    20→Do NOT attempt to generate images through any other method.
    21→
    22→## Before First Use
    23→
    24→1. Verify extension is installed:
    25→   ```bash
    26→   gemini extensions list | grep nanobanana
    27→   ```
    28→2. If missing, install it:
    29→   ```bash
    30→   gemini extensions install https://github.com/gemini-cli-extensions/nanobanana
    31→   ```
    32→3. Verify API key is set:
    33→   ```bash
    34→   [ -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" ] && echo "API key configured" || echo "Missing GEMINI_API_KEY"
    35→   ```
    36→
    37→## Command Selection
    38→
    39→| User Request | Command |
    40→|--------------|---------|
    41→| "make me a blog header" | `/generate` |
    42→| "create an app icon" | `/icon` |
    43→| "draw a flowchart of..." | `/diagram` |
    44→| "fix this old photo" | `/restore` |
    45→| "remove the background" | `/edit` |
    46→| "create a repeating texture" | `/pattern` |
    47→| "make a comic strip" | `/story` |
    48→
    49→## Available Commands
    50→
    51→**Note:** Always use the `--yolo` flag to automatically approve all tool actions.
    52→
    53→| Command | Use Case |
    54→|---------|----------|
    55→| `gemini --yolo "/generate 'prompt'"` | Text-to-image generation |
    56→| `gemini --yolo "/edit file.png 'instruction'"` | Modify existing image |
    57→| `gemini --yolo "/restore old_photo.jpg 'fix scratches'"` | Repair damaged photos |
    58→| `gemini --yolo "/icon 'description'"` | App icons, favicons, UI elements |
    59→| `gemini --yolo "/diagram 'description'"` | Flowcharts, architecture diagrams |
    60→| `gemini --yolo "/pattern 'description'"` | Seamless textures and patterns |
    61→| `gemini --yolo "/story 'description'"` | Sequential/narrative images |
    62→| `gemini --yolo "/nanobanana prompt"` | Natural language interface |
    63→
    64→## Common Options
    65→
    66→- `--yolo` - **Required.** Auto-approve all tool actions (no confirmation prompts)
    67→- `--count=N` - Generate N variations (1-8)
    68→- `--preview` - Auto-open generated images
    69→- `--styles="style1,style2"` - Apply artistic styles
    70→- `--format=grid|separate` - Output arrangement
    71→
    72→## Common Sizes
    73→
    74→| Use Case | Dimensions | Notes |
    75→|----------|------------|-------|
    76→| YouTube thumbnail | 1280x720 | `--aspect=16:9` |
    77→| Blog featured image | 1200x630 | Social preview friendly |
    78→| Square social | 1080x1080 | Instagram, LinkedIn |
    79→| Twitter/X header | 1500x500 | Wide banner |
    80→| Vertical story | 1080x1920 | `--aspect=9:16` |
    81→
    82→## Model Selection
    83→
    84→Default: `gemini-2.5-flash-image` (~$0.04/image)
    85→
    86→For higher quality (4K, better reasoning):
    87→```bash
    88→export NANOBANANA_MODEL=gemini-3-pro-image-preview
    89→```
    90→
    91→## Blog Featured Image Examples
    92→
    93→```bash
    94→# Modern illustration style
    95→gemini --yolo "/generate 'modern flat illustration of developer coding at laptop, purple and blue gradient background, minimalist style, no text' --preview"
    96→
    97→# Professional photography style
    98→gemini --yolo "/generate 'professional editorial photo of coffee cup next to laptop on wooden desk, morning sunlight, shallow depth of field, no text' --count=3"
    99→
   100→# Tech/abstract
   101→gemini --yolo "/generate 'abstract visualization of neural network connections, dark background with glowing blue nodes, futuristic style' --preview"
   102→```
   103→
   104→## Icon Generation
   105→
   106→```bash
   107→gemini --yolo "/icon 'minimalist app logo for productivity tool' --sizes='64,128,256,512' --type='app-icon' --corners='rounded'"
   108→```
   109→
   110→## Diagram Generation
   111→
   112→```bash
   113→gemini --yolo "/diagram 'user authentication flow with OAuth' --type='flowchart' --style='modern'"
   114→```
   115→
   116→## Output Location
   117→
   118→All generated images are saved to `./nanobanana-output/` in the current directory.
   119→
   120→## Presenting Results
   121→
   122→After generation completes:
   123→1. List contents of `./nanobanana-output/` to find generated files
   124→2. Present the most recent image(s) to the user
   125→3. Offer to regenerate with variations if needed
   126→
   127→## Refinements and Iterations
   128→
   129→When the user asks for changes:
   130→- **"Try again" / "Give me options"**: Regenerate with `--count=3`
   131→- **"Make it more [adjective]"**: Adjust prompt and regenerate
   132→- **"Edit this one"**: Use `gemini --yolo "/edit nanobanana-output/filename.png 'adjustment'"`
   133→- **"Different style"**: Add `--styles="requested_style"` to the command
   134→
   135→## Prompt Tips
   136→
   137→1. **Be specific**: Include style, mood, colors, composition details
   138→2. **Add "no text"**: If you don't want text rendered in the image
   139→3. **Reference styles**: "editorial photography", "flat illustration", "3D render", "watercolor"
   140→4. **Specify aspect ratio context**: "wide banner", "square thumbnail", "vertical story"
   141→
   142→## Troubleshooting
   143→
   144→| Problem | Solution |
   145→|---------|----------|
   146→| `GEMINI_API_KEY` not set | `export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-key"` |
   147→| Extension not found | Run install command from setup section |
   148→| Quota exceeded | Wait for reset or switch to flash model |
   149→| Image generation failed | Check prompt for policy violations, simplify request |
   150→| Output directory missing | Will be created automatically on first run |

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1→# Neo4j Cypher Query Helper — gpsl-cp-system
     2→
     3→You are a Cypher query expert for the gpsl-cp-system Neo4j graph database. When invoked, help the user write, debug, optimize, or understand Cypher queries against this specific graph.
     4→
     5→## Behavior
     6→
     7→- Always work from the schema below. Do not guess node labels or relationship types.
     8→- If the user's request is ambiguous, ask a clarifying question before writing a query.
     9→- Prefer parameterized queries (e.g., `$param`) over literals.
    10→- Warn when a query will perform a full graph scan (no index hit on the anchor node).
    11→- Suggest `EXPLAIN` or `PROFILE` when optimizing an existing query.
    12→- When writing queries for NestJS, format them as template strings suitable for `neo4j-driver` session.run() or the `nest-neo4j` query helper.
    13→- Project query files live in `discovery-services/libs/discovery-neo/src/*/[domain].queries.ts`.
    14→
    15→---
    16→
    17→## Node Labels & Properties
    18→
    19→### Document
    20→| Property   | Type   | Constraint |
    21→|------------|--------|------------|
    22→| sourceId   | string | UNIQUE, indexed |
    23→| name       | string | indexed |
    24→| createdAt  | string | indexed |
    25→
    26→### Text
    27→| Property        | Type   | Constraint |
    28→|-----------------|--------|------------|
    29→| sourceId        | string | UNIQUE, indexed |
    30→| guid            | string | UNIQUE, indexed |
    31→| pageNumber      | number | indexed |
    32→| paragraphNumber | number | indexed |
    33→| text            | string | |
    34→| nodeType        | string | |
    35→| nodePath        | string | |
    36→
    37→### Collection
    38→| Property  | Type   | Constraint |
    39→|-----------|--------|------------|
    40→| sourceId  | string | UNIQUE |
    41→| name      | string | |
    42→| createdAt | string | |
    43→
    44→### GlossaryTerm
    45→| Property    | Type   | Constraint |
    46→|-------------|--------|------------|
    47→| id          | string | UNIQUE |
    48→| name        | string | indexed |
    49→| wordnv      | string | |
    50→| acronym     | string | |
    51→| posnv       | string | |
    52→| tag         | string | |
    53→| meaning     | string | |
    54→| category    | string | |
    55→| taxonomy_l1 | string | |
    56→| taxonomy_l2 | string | |
    57→| taxonomy_l3 | string | |
    58→
    59→### Terminology
    60→| Property | Type   | Constraint |
    61→|----------|--------|------------|
    62→| sourceId | string | |
    63→| name     | string | |
    64→
    65→### Acronym
    66→| Property | Type   | Constraint |
    67→|----------|--------|------------|
    68→| id       | string | UNIQUE |
    69→| name     | string | indexed |
    70→| acronym  | string | |
    71→| meaning  | string | |
    72→
    73→### Other Referenced Labels
    74→- `Date` — limited detail, proceed with caution
    75→- `Requirement` — limited detail, proceed with caution
    76→- `Temporal` — limited detail, proceed with caution
    77→
    78→---
    79→
    80→## Relationship Types
    81→
    82→```
    83→(Collection)-[:HAS_DOCUMENT]->(Document)
    84→(Document)-[:HAS_TEXT]->(Text)
    85→(Collection)-[:USES]->(Terminology)
    86→(Text)-[:CONTAINS]->(GlossaryTerm)
    87→(GlossaryTerm)-[:HAS_ACRONYM]->(Acronym)
    88→(GlossaryTerm)-[:IS_PART_OF]->(Terminology)
    89→(Acronym)-[:IS_PART_OF]->(Terminology)
    90→```
    91→
    92→---
    93→
    94→## Common Patterns
    95→
    96→### Find all Text nodes in a Document
    97→```cypher
    98→MATCH (d:Document {sourceId: $sourceId})-[:HAS_TEXT]->(t:Text)
    99→RETURN t ORDER BY t.pageNumber, t.paragraphNumber
   100→```
   101→
   102→### Find GlossaryTerms in a Collection via text
   103→```cypher
   104→MATCH (c:Collection {sourceId: $collectionId})-[:HAS_DOCUMENT]->(d:Document)
   105→      -[:HAS_TEXT]->(t:Text)-[:CONTAINS]->(gt:GlossaryTerm)
   106→RETURN DISTINCT gt
   107→```
   108→
   109→### Find all terms under a Terminology
   110→```cypher
   111→MATCH (gt:GlossaryTerm)-[:IS_PART_OF]->(term:Terminology {name: $termName})
   112→RETURN gt
   113→```
   114→
   115→### Find acronym expansion
   116→```cypher
   117→MATCH (gt:GlossaryTerm)-[:HAS_ACRONYM]->(a:Acronym)
   118→WHERE a.acronym = $acronym
   119→RETURN gt.name, a.meaning
   120→```
   121→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1→# PostgreSQL Query Helper — gpsl-cp-system
     2→
     3→You are a PostgreSQL query expert for the gpsl-cp-system database. When invoked, help the user write, debug, optimize, or understand SQL queries against this specific schema.
     4→
     5→## Behavior
     6→
     7→- Always work from the schema below. Do not guess table or column names.
     8→- If the user's request is ambiguous, ask a clarifying question before writing a query.
     9→- Prefer parameterized queries over string interpolation.
    10→- Suggest `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` when optimizing an existing query.
    11→- Warn when a query will result in a sequential scan on a large table (missing index).
    12→- When writing queries for NestJS, note the tradeoff between TypeORM QueryBuilder and raw SQL — prefer QueryBuilder for maintainability unless raw SQL is clearly necessary.
    13→- Entity files live in `discovery-services/libs/discovery-database/src/entities/`.
    14→- Migration files live in `discovery-services/libs/discovery-database/src/migrations/`.
    15→- The `text_chunks` table uses **pgvector** — vector similarity queries use the `<->` operator.
    16→
    17→---
    18→
    19→## Schema
    20→
    21→### users
    22→| Column        | Type         | Constraints          |
    23→|---------------|--------------|----------------------|
    24→| id            | SERIAL       | PRIMARY KEY          |
    25→| username      | VARCHAR(255) | UNIQUE, NOT NULL     |
    26→| password_hash | VARCHAR(255) | NOT NULL             |
    27→| is_active     | BOOLEAN      | DEFAULT true         |
    28→| created_at    | TIMESTAMP    |                      |
    29→| updated_at    | TIMESTAMP    |                      |
    30→
    31→### user_credentials
    32→| Column                  | Type         | Constraints                        |
    33→|-------------------------|--------------|------------------------------------|
    34→| id                      | SERIAL       | PRIMARY KEY                        |
    35→| user_id                 | INT          | FK → users.id ON DELETE CASCADE    |
    36→| neo4j_username          | VARCHAR(255) |                                    |
    37→| neo4j_password_encrypted| TEXT         |                                    |
    38→| neo4j_uri               | VARCHAR(255) | DEFAULT 'bolt://localhost:7687'    |
    39→| neo4j_database          | VARCHAR(255) | DEFAULT 'neo4j'                    |
    40→| created_at              | TIMESTAMP    |                                    |
    41→| updated_at              | TIMESTAMP    |                                    |
    42→
    43→Indexes: `user_id`
    44→
    45→### enrichment_transactions
    46→| Column               | Type         | Constraints       |
    47→|----------------------|--------------|-------------------|
    48→| id                   | VARCHAR(36)  | PRIMARY KEY       |
    49→| type                 | VARCHAR(50)  |                   |
    50→| status               | VARCHAR(50)  |                   |
    51→| priority             | INT          | DEFAULT 5         |
    52→| document_id          | VARCHAR(255) |                   |
    53→| request_data         | TEXT         |                   |
    54→| result               | TEXT         |                   |
    55→| error                | TEXT         |                   |
    56→| callback_url         | VARCHAR(255) |                   |
    57→| created_at           | TIMESTAMP    |                   |
    58→| updated_at           | TIMESTAMP    |                   |
    59→| has_rollback_points  | BOOLEAN      | DEFAULT false     |
    60→| was_rolled_back      | BOOLEAN      | DEFAULT false     |
    61→| rollback_timestamp   | TIMESTAMP    |                   |
    62→| rollback_reason      | TEXT         |                   |
    63→| cleanup_performed    | BOOLEAN      | DEFAULT false     |
    64→| cleanup_timestamp    | TIMESTAMP    |                   |
    65→| created_by           | VARCHAR(255) |                   |
    66→| api_key_id           | VARCHAR(255) |                   |
    67→| client_ip            | VARCHAR(45)  |                   |
    68→
    69→Indexes: `status`, `document_id`, `created_at`, `has_rollback_points`
    70→
    71→### enrichment_logs
    72→| Column         | Type        | Constraints                                       |
    73→|----------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------------|
    74→| id             | SERIAL      | PRIMARY KEY                                       |
    75→| transaction_id | VARCHAR(36) | FK → enrichment_transactions.id ON DELETE CASCADE |
    76→| message        | TEXT        |                                                   |
    77→| level          | VARCHAR(20) |                                                   |
    78→| timestamp      | TIMESTAMP   |                                                   |
    79→| severity       | VARCHAR(20) | DEFAULT 'MEDIUM'                                  |
    80→| context        | JSONB       |                                                   |
    81→
    82→Indexes: `transaction_id`, `timestamp`, `severity`, composite `(level, timestamp)`
    83→
    84→### enrichment_rollback_points
    85→| Column          | Type        | Constraints                                       |
    86→|-----------------|-------------|---------------------------------------------------|
    87→| id              | SERIAL      | PRIMARY KEY                                       |
    88→| transaction_id  | VARCHAR(36) | FK → enrichment_transactions.id ON DELETE CASCADE |
    89→| batch_id        | VARCHAR(36) |                                                   |
    90→| document_id     | VARCHAR(255)|                                                   |
    91→| batch_number    | INT         |                                                   |
    92→| rollback_point  | INT         |                                                   |
    93→| processed_count | INT         |                                                   |
    94→| success_count   | INT         |                                                   |
    95→| error_count     | INT         |                                                   |
    96→| created_at      | TIMESTAMP   |                                                   |
    97→
    98→Indexes: `transaction_id`, `document_id`, `created_at`
    99→
   100→### enrichment_handler_priorities
   101→| Column       | Type         | Constraints             |
   102→|--------------|--------------|-------------------------|
   103→| id           | SERIAL       | PRIMARY KEY             |
   104→| handler_name | VARCHAR(100) | UNIQUE                  |
   105→| priority     | INT          | CHECK (1–10)            |
   106→| updated_by   | VARCHAR(100) |                         |
   107→| created_at   | TIMESTAMP    | DEFAULT NOW()           |
   108→| updated_at   | TIMESTAMP    | DEFAULT NOW()           |
   109→
   110→Note: `updated_at` is auto-updated via trigger on row modification.
   111→
   112→### transaction_audit_log
   113→| Column         | Type          | Constraints                                       |
   114→|----------------|---------------|---------------------------------------------------|
   115→| id             | BIGSERIAL     | PRIMARY KEY                                       |
   116→| transaction_id | VARCHAR(36)   | FK → enrichment_transactions.id ON DELETE CASCADE |
   117→| user_id        | VARCHAR(255)  |                                                   |
   118→| api_key_id     | VARCHAR(255)  |                                                   |
   119→| action_type    | VARCHAR(50)   |                                                   |
   120→| old_values     | JSONB         |                                                   |
   121→| new_values     | JSONB         |                                                   |
   122→| ip_address     | VARCHAR(45)   |                                                   |
   123→| user_agent     | TEXT          |                                                   |
   124→| timestamp      | TIMESTAMP(6)  | DEFAULT NOW()                                     |
   125→| checksum       | VARCHAR(64)   |                                                   |
   126→
   127→Indexes: `transaction_id`, `timestamp`, `user_id`, `action_type`, composite `(user_id, action_type, timestamp)`, `checksum`
   128→
   129→### text_chunks
   130→| Column             | Type          | Constraints   |
   131→|--------------------|---------------|---------------|
   132→| id                 | VARCHAR       | PRIMARY KEY   |
   133→| source_document_id | VARCHAR       |               |
   134→| text               | TEXT          |               |
   135→| embedding          | vector(1536)  | pgvector      |
   136→| created_at         | TIMESTAMP     |               |
   137→
   138→Indexes: `source_document_id`
   139→
   140→> Vector similarity query pattern: `ORDER BY embedding <-> $queryEmbedding LIMIT 10`
   141→
   142→### transactions-ds
   143→| Column     | Type      | Constraints        |
   144→|------------|-----------|--------------------|
   145→| id         | VARCHAR   | PRIMARY KEY        |
   146→| state      | VARCHAR   | DEFAULT 'CREATED'  |
   147→| created_at | TIMESTAMP |                    |
   148→| updated_at | TIMESTAMP |                    |
   149→| metadata   | JSONB     |                    |
   150→| error      | VARCHAR   |                    |
   151→
   152→Indexes: `state`
   153→
   154→### email_queue  *(email-daemon service)*
   155→| Column       | Type        | Constraints     |
   156→|--------------|-------------|-----------------|
   157→| id           | SERIAL      | PRIMARY KEY     |
   158→| eventType    | VARCHAR(50) |                 |
   159→| fromEmailId  | VARCHAR     |                 |
   160→| subject      | VARCHAR     |                 |
   161→| content      | TEXT        |                 |
   162→| isHtml       | BOOLEAN     | DEFAULT true    |
   163→| status       | VARCHAR     | DEFAULT 'pending'|
   164→| sentOn       | TIMESTAMP   |                 |
   165→| requestId    | VARCHAR     |                 |
   166→| retryCount   | INT         | DEFAULT 0       |
   167→| errorMessage | VARCHAR     |                 |
   168→| createdOn    | TIMESTAMP   | DEFAULT NOW()   |
   169→| createdBy    | VARCHAR     |                 |
   170→
   171→---
   172→
   173→## Foreign Key Map
   174→
   175→```
   176→user_credentials.user_id             → users.id
   177→enrichment_logs.transaction_id       → enrichment_transactions.id
   178→enrichment_rollback_points.transaction_id → enrichment_transactions.id
   179→transaction_audit_log.transaction_id → enrichment_transactions.id
   180→```
   181→
   182→---
   183→
   184→## Common Patterns
   185→
   186→### Fetch a transaction with its logs
   187→```sql
   188→SELECT t.*, l.message, l.level, l.severity, l.timestamp AS log_time
   189→FROM enrichment_transactions t
   190→JOIN enrichment_logs l ON l.transaction_id = t.id
   191→WHERE t.id = $1
   192→ORDER BY l.timestamp;
   193→```
   194→
   195→### Pending transactions by priority
   196→```sql
   197→SELECT * FROM enrichment_transactions
   198→WHERE status = 'PENDING'
   199→ORDER BY priority DESC, created_at ASC;
   200→```
   201→
   202→### Vector similarity search (pgvector)
   203→```sql
   204→SELECT id, source_document_id, text,
   205→       embedding <-> $1 AS distance
   206→FROM text_chunks
   207→ORDER BY embedding <-> $1
   208→LIMIT 10;
   209→```
   210→
   211→### Audit trail for a transaction
   212→```sql
   213→SELECT user_id, action_type, old_values, new_values, timestamp
   214→FROM transaction_audit_log
   215→WHERE transaction_id = $1
   216→ORDER BY timestamp;
   217→```
   218→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

content.text:
## Summary of Existing Skills

I've found three skill files across both directories. Here's what exists:

### Directory 1: `C:\Users\mbrow\.claude\skills\`

**File:** `C:\Users\mbrow\.claude\skills\nano-banana\SKILL.md`
- **Skill Name:** `nano-banana`
- **Purpose:** Image generation and editing using Gemini CLI's nanobanana extension
- **Triggers:** When user asks to create/generate/edit any image, graphic, illustration, visual content
- **Commands:** `/generate`, `/icon`, `/diagram`, `/restore`, `/edit`, `/pattern`, `/story`
- **Allowed Tools:** `Bash(gemini:*)`
- **Key Features:**
  - Blog featured images, thumbnails, icons, diagrams
  - Photo restoration and editing
  - Size recommendations for different platforms (YouTube, Instagram, Twitter, etc.)
  - Model selection (default: gemini-2.5-flash-image)
  - Output to `./nanobanana-output/`

---

### Directory 2: `C:\Users\mbrow\git\gpsl-cp-system\.claude\skills\`

**File:** `C:\Users\mbrow\git\gpsl-cp-system\.claude\skills\neo4j-query.md`
- **Skill Name:** `neo4j-query` (inferred from filename)
- **Purpose:** Neo4j Cypher query expert for gpsl-cp-system graph database
- **Scope:** Writing, debugging, optimizing Cypher queries
- **Key Content:**
  - Node labels: Document, Text, Collection, GlossaryTerm, Terminology, Acronym
  - Relationship types and their patterns
  - Common query patterns (find text in documents, glossary terms, acronyms, etc.)
  - Schema reference with constraints and indexes
  - Optimized query suggestions

**File:** `C:\Users\mbrow\git\gpsl-cp-system\.claude\skills\postgres-query.md`
- **Skill Name:** `postgres-query` (inferred from filename)
